sentence: Subjective:

HPI: Mr. Jackson is a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presenting with right elbow pain that has been ongoing for the past three weeks, exacerbated by playing tennis.
The pain is described as sharp, stabbing, and aching, and is worse with movement and better with rest.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they have not provided significant relief.
He has also tried icing the area, but it has not helped much.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma.
He is currently taking metformin 500 milligrams twice a day for his diabetes, and has a known allergy to penicillin.
He has a surgical history of having his gallbladder removed 10 years ago.
Review of systems: No other symptoms mentioned.
Current medications:
Metformin 500 milligrams twice a day
Flovent twice a day
Albuterol as needed
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam:
Moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow
Pain with passive range of motion of the right elbow
Mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist
Normal cardiovascular exam, except for a grade 3/6 systolic ejection murmur
Test Results:
Right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, no malalignment or joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increased tennis activity.
The patient's blood sugar is a bit out of control, and he needs adjustments to his diabetes management.
His asthma is well-controlled.
Problem list:
Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow; diabetes type two; mild intermittent asthma
Plan:
I prescribed ultram 50 milligrams once per day for the elbow pain.
I recommended that the patient rest their right arm as much as possible, avoid repetitive stress in that arm, and ice the right elbow for 20 minutes at least three times a day.
I also prescribed a brace for the right forearm to help with discomfort.
I increased the patient's metformin to 1000 milligrams twice a day and instructed him to check his blood sugar twice daily for the next two weeks.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, a CBC blood test, and a chem-12 blood test.
I also wrote a prescription for a refill of albuterol.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen in four weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
